full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Identifying the right baselines to compare against DynaMMo
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: What are the two most appropriate baseline methods to compare DynaMMo against for missing-value imputation in multivariate time series? I want baselines that are (a) commonly used in practice, (b) simple enough to implement in a single function, but (c) genuinely informative about what DynaMMo adds. Explain what each baseline captures and what it fails to capture.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Confirmed column-mean fill and linear interpolation as the right choices. Mean fill captures the unconditional average but ignores all temporal structure. Linear interpolation captures local temporal continuity but no cross-sensor or global structure. Together they isolate DynaMMo's two advantages.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified these are the same baselines used in the paper's Table 1. Implemented both independently using numpy and pandas — no LLM code used.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Identifying a condition where DynaMMo would fail to beat the baselines
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Under what specific data conditions would DynaMMo fail to outperform simple linear interpolation for missing-value imputation? I want a precise, testable condition — not a vague statement about 'model mismatch'.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Identified high structured missing rates as the testable failure condition — specifically when the missing pattern is correlated (sensors fail together) rather than random. This gave me the precise formulation for the Task 1.3 'failure condition' field.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Confirmed by running the Task 3.2 experiment which empirically shows DynaMMo losing to linear interpolation at 30%-60% structured missing rates.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Framing the comparison in terms of which assumption each method relies on
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Can you frame the comparison between DynaMMo, mean fill, and linear interpolation in terms of the data assumptions each method implicitly makes? I want to understand the hierarchy of assumptions from weakest to strongest.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: The hierarchy (mean fill assumes stationarity only; linear interpolation adds temporal smoothness; DynaMMo additionally assumes low-rank LDS structure and MCAR) was exactly the analytical framing I used in the comparison section of Task 1.3.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Verified the assumption hierarchy against my own understanding of the methods. Each assumption is independently verifiable from the method's definition.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding why mean RMSE improvement varies so much across datasets
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: The DynaMMo paper shows 20-40% RMSE improvement over mean fill on real datasets. My synthetic reproduction shows 68%. Why would a synthetic dataset show larger improvement than real data, even if both use the same algorithm and similar missing rates?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Three reasons identified: (1) zero model mismatch on synthetic data, (2) smaller T means cross-sequence signal is proportionally stronger, (3) real data has non-Gaussian noise. This became my 'honest commentary' in Task 4.1 and Task 2.3.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Reasoned through each point independently. Point 1 is definitional (synthetic = exact LDS). Point 3 was verified by checking that neither UCI Chlorine nor CMU Motion Capture are generated by Gaussian processes.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Identifying a more competitive baseline that would be fair to include
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Beyond mean fill and linear interpolation, is there a third baseline that would be a fairer comparison for DynaMMo — one that also uses the multivariate structure but without a probabilistic model? Something that a practitioner would realistically try before DynaMMo?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Matrix factorisation (e.g., SVD-based imputation) was identified as the natural competitor — it exploits low-rank structure like DynaMMo but without temporal dynamics. I noted this as a 'limitation' in the Task 1.3 comparison: our reproduction does not include MF as a baseline.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: Confirmed that matrix factorisation would use the same low-rank assumption as DynaMMo (shared latent structure) but ignore time. This is an honest gap in the baseline comparison.

top_5_prompts.prompt: What are the two most appropriate baseline methods to compare DynaMMo against? I want baselines that are commonly used in practice, simple to implement, but genuinely informative about what DynaMMo adds.
top_5_prompts.why_important: Confirmed the right baseline choices and gave me the analytical framing of what each baseline captures — directly shaped the comparison structure in Task 1.3.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Under what specific data conditions would DynaMMo fail to outperform simple linear interpolation? I want a precise, testable condition.
top_5_prompts.why_important: Produced the exact failure condition (high structured missing rate) that I implemented as the Task 3.2 experiment — a direct bridge from theory to experiment.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Can you frame the comparison between DynaMMo, mean fill, and linear interpolation in terms of the data assumptions each method implicitly makes, from weakest to strongest?
top_5_prompts.why_important: The assumption hierarchy became the analytical backbone of the Task 1.3 comparison — explaining why DynaMMo wins under some conditions but not others.

top_5_prompts.why_important: The three-reason explanation (zero model mismatch, smaller T, real-data noise) became my honest commentary in both Task 1.3 and Task 4.1.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Is there a third baseline that would be a fairer comparison for DynaMMo — one that also uses the multivariate structure but without a probabilistic model?

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Part B.
